content:
Rule ID: CON33-C

Category: CON

Description:
Avoid race conditions when using library functions by ensuring that shared resources are accessed in a thread-safe manner. Library functions are not always reentrant or thread-safe, so protect calls to such functions with appropriate synchronization mechanisms to prevent concurrent access that can lead to inconsistent or corrupted data.

Fix:
Use mutexes or disable interrupts around library function calls that access shared resources to ensure exclusive access, especially in embedded or RTOS-based STM32 applications.

Rule ID: MSC40-C

Category: MSC

Description:
Do not violate constraints imposed by the language or the implementation, such as accessing memory out of bounds, using uninitialized variables, or exceeding hardware limitations. Such violations can lead to undefined behavior, system crashes, or security vulnerabilities.

Fix:
Validate all inputs, initialize variables before use, and enforce memory boundaries using static or dynamic checks appropriate for embedded environments.

Rule ID: ERR33-C

Category: ERR

Description:
Detect and properly handle error indications returned by standard library functions to avoid undefined behavior or security vulnerabilities caused by ignoring errors.

Fix:
Check return values of standard library functions, handle errors immediately by implementing recovery, cleanup, or safe fallback procedures suitable for STM32 embedded environments.

Rule ID: DCL40-C

Category: DCL

Description:
Do not create incompatible declarations of the same function or object. Incompatible declarations can lead to undefined behavior due to mismatched types, calling conventions, or storage durations. Ensure all declarations and definitions consistently use the same type, qualifier, and linkage.

Fix:
Ensure all declarations and definitions of a function or object use exactly the same type, qualifiers, and linkage specifications. In STM32 projects, declare functions in header files with matching prototypes and include these headers consistently across source files.

Rule ID: DCL31-C

Category: DCL

Description:
All identifiers must be declared before their first use to ensure proper type checking, avoid implicit declarations, and prevent undefined behavior.

Fix:
Declare all variables and functions before their first usage, typically at the beginning of a block or in appropriate header files.

Rule ID: MSC32-C

Category: MSC

Description:
Properly seed pseudorandom number generators (PRNGs) using sources of sufficient entropy before generating random values to ensure unpredictable sequences and prevent security vulnerabilities arising from predictable pseudorandom numbers.

Fix:
Use a high-entropy seed such as a hardware RNG, unique device ID, or timer-based value to initialize the PRNG before generating random numbers, e.g., srand((unsigned int)HAL_GetTick()); for STM32.

Rule ID: MSC38-C

Category: MSC

Description:
Do not treat a predefined identifier as an object if it might only be defined as a macro or a compilerIntrinsic, to avoid undefined behavior or conflicts.

Fix:
Avoid declaring variables or objects with names matching predefined identifiers; use distinct names or enclosed preprocessing directives to differentiate.

Rule ID: ARR30-C

Category: ARR

Description:
Do not form or use pointers or array subscripts that point outside the bounds of the intended object. Accessing memory outside array bounds causes undefined behavior and may lead to system instability or security vulnerabilities.

Fix:
Ensure all pointer arithmetic and array indices remain within valid bounds by validating indices against array sizes before access.

Rule ID: SIG31-C

Category: SIG

Description:
Do not access or modify shared objects in signal handlers, as signal handlers can interrupt the main program flow at unpredictable points, leading to data races or inconsistent state if shared objects are accessed without synchronization.

Fix:
Use atomic operations or set a flag of type volatile sig_atomic_t in the signal handler and handle the shared object outside the handler to ensure safe access.

Rule ID: EXP33-C

Category: EXP

Description:
Do not read uninitialized memory, as reading uninitialized variables leads to undefined behavior and may result in unpredictable program operation or security vulnerabilities.

Fix:
Always initialize variables before use, preferably by zeroing memory or assigning a known initial value immediately after declaration.

Rule ID: MSC30-C

Category: MSC

Description:
Do not use the rand() function for generating pseudorandom numbers as it often produces low-quality randomness, has implementation-defined behavior, and may be unsuitable for security-sensitive or high-quality random number requirements.

Fix:
Use hardware random number generators (e.g., STM32 RNG peripheral) or implement a well-tested cryptographic PRNG instead of rand() for better randomness and predictability.

Rule ID: CON32-C

Category: CON

Description:
Prevent data races when accessing bit-fields from multiple threads. Bit-fields in C are not guaranteed to be accessed atomically, which can result in unpredictable behavior in concurrent environments. To ensure thread safety, concurrent access to bit-fields must be properly synchronized or avoided.

Fix:
Protect all accesses to shared bit-fields with mutexes or disable interrupts during access to ensure atomicity.

Rule ID: PRE32-C

Category: PRE

Description:
Do not use preprocessor directives (such as #if, #ifdef, #ifndef) within the arguments of function-like macros, as this can lead to unexpected behavior and code that is difficult to debug and maintain.

Fix:
Perform conditional checks outside the macro invocation and pass only fully resolved arguments to the function-like macro.

Rule ID: ARR38-C

Category: ARR

Description:
Ensure that pointers returned by library functions are valid and do not point outside the allocated object or become invalid due to overflow, underflow, or arithmetic on pointers, to prevent undefined behavior and potential security vulnerabilities.

Fix:
Validate pointers returned by library functions before use; check that they fall within the bounds of the allocated memory region and avoid arithmetic that produces pointers outside valid ranges.

Rule ID: CON37-C

Category: CON

Description:
Do not call signal() in a multithreaded program because signal handlers and signal() calls are not thread-safe and may lead to undefined behavior. Use timer-based interrupts or dedicated thread-safe event handling mechanisms instead.

Fix:
Avoid using signal() in multithreaded code; implement hardware interrupts or RTOS-native event flags or message queues to handle asynchronous events safely.
